description: Apollo 10 Flight Journal - Day 4, part 16: Rest and Preparation for Solo Operations

tokens: We don't expect any further conversation with the crew on the pass, In the event that we do hear anything, we'll come back up and play it back for you.
At the present time Apollo 10 is about 48 minutes from Loss Of Signal, at the point at which it will be going behind the Moon on this the sixth revolution.
And we're 87 hours, 22 seconds now into the mission.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 87 hours, 47 minutes.
We're about 1 minute, 20 seconds now from LOS and it appears that all three crewmen are asleep at this time.
We have about 5½ hours of sleep period left.

At present time the spacecraft is in an orbit 61 nautical miles (113 km) by 59 nautical miles (109 km) and our velocity is about 5,343 feet per second (1,629 meters per second), or about 1 mile per second.
We are now 30 seconds from Loss Of Signal as the spacecraft approaches the end of its sixth revolution.
And after having observed all spacecraft systems on both the LM and the CSM, flight controllers here in Mission Control have concluded that everything is continuing to function normally.
And we are now showing Loss Of Signal.
We'll reacquire Apollo 10 in 46 minutes at which time we'll get another look at all the spacecraft systems.
Continuing to monitor the performance of bothSnoopyandCharlie Brown.
At 87 hours, 49 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 begins its seventh lunar orbit at about 088:05:00.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 88 hours, 32 minutes now into the flight of Apollo 10.
We're 1 minute, 50 seconds from reacquiring Apollo 10 as the spacecraft comes around the front of the Moon on its seventh revolution.
We don't anticipate any communication with the crew.
It appears that they are all asleep.
At least that was the indication we had prior to losing signal from the spacecraft on the previous revolution.
We will stand by for Acquisition Of Signal and we'll listen briefly, and in case we should get any call from the spacecraft.
We're now 30 seconds from reacquiring Apollo 10 and here in Mission Control we're also in the midst of a change of shift.
Flight Director Pete Frank and his Orange Team of flight controllers coming on to replace the Maroon Team headed by Flight Director Milton Windler.
And we're still standing by for confirmation from the network controller that we do have acquisition of data from the spacecraft.
And we've been advised by the network controller that we do have data from the spacecraft at this time.
It's now 5 minutes, 10 seconds into the front-side pass on this, the seventh revolution around the Moon.
We'll have Loss Of Signal in 1 hour, 7 minutes approximately from now.
And we'll continue to monitor spacecraft systems and stand by, should the crew awake and decide to put in a call to the control center here.
We have about 4½ hours left in the present rest cycle.
And all indications at this time are that the crew are all sleeping.
A change of shift press briefing is scheduled.
We expect that will occur in about 20 minutes at about 10 minutes to five Central Daylight Time.
At 88 hours, 41 minutes; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This thing ain't going to be shut.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: [Yawning] That was the finest sleep I could get.
Have you got all that information?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 89 hours, 47 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 10 spacecraft has just gone over the hill.
The end - toward the end of the seventh lunar revolution.
We have some 44 minutes, 15 seconds until Acquisition Of Signal again.
Lunar orbit measurements are 61.5 nautical mile (113.9 km) apogee, 58.7 nautical mile (108.7 km) perigee.
As you were - those are apocynthion and pericynthion respectively to get into lunar terminology rather than Earth terminology.
And it shows the next revolution Acquisition Of Signal at 92 hours, 32 minutes, 25 seconds.
The Orange Team of flight controllers have settled in for tonight's activities.
Coming up on manning of the Lunar Module, preparations for today's rendezvous sequence.
And at 89 hours, 49 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 begins its eighth lunar orbit at about 089:55:00
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 90 hours, 32 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've just had Acquisition Of Signal as Apollo 10 came over the hill beginning the 8th revolution around the Moon.

The crew has a little less than two hours remaining in the sleep period before beginning a very busy day; powering up, manning the Lunar Module and doing the complete rendezvous sequence which will include a sweep down within 8 nautical miles [15 km] of the lunar surface quite near Apollo landing site number 2.
We have an hour and 11 minutes remaining in this revolution before Loss Of Signal.
If the crew should waken and begin talking to the ground we'll come up again with the circuit.
And at 90 hours, 33 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 91 hours, 44 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've just had Loss Of Signal from Apollo 10 as it went over the hill on the 8th revolution - lunar revolution.
The spacecraft systems are functioning extremely well.
In fact, one of the shortest hourly reports on record has just come into the Control Center from the spacecraft analysis staff support room, about a half a page long.
And most of the entries say systems performance normal.
No change in status, and so on.

As the spacecraft went over the hill, Commander - Apollo 10 Commander Tom Stafford had a heart rate of about 53, respiration rate 8; the Command Module Pilot John Young had a heart rate of 57, respiration 11; cabin pressure holding at 4.9 pounds per square inch; cabin temperature 77 degrees Fahrenheit (25 degrees Centigrade); orbital weight now calculated at 68,144 pounds (30,910 kg).
Apollo 10 is in a 58.4 by 61.7 nautical mile (108.2 x 114.3 km) lunar orbit.
One hour and 25 minutes until alarm, wakeup alarm time.
We'll have Acquisition Of Signal again at 92 hours, 30 minutes through Madrid, which is just less than an hour from now.
At 91 hours, 46 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 begins its seventh lunar orbit at about 091:50:00.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 92 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We've just had Acquisition Of Signal from Apollo 10 as it came around from the back side of the Moon on the 9th revolution.
The nominal awake time is some 40 minutes away.
However, if it appears the crew is awake at the present time, we'll begin the day's activities.
The flight surgeon will sample the incoming biomedical data to try to determine whether they indeed are awake.
From heart rate, respiration and so on, he can tell pretty well what a man's status is.
No call from the crew yet.

After the crew has awakened, their first job of course will be to have breakfast, report their crew status on sleep to the ground here in Mission Control Center, get a Flight Plan update on the day's activities, and prepare to repressurize the Lunar Module and the Lunar Module Pilot will be the first man across into the LM through the tunnel, at about 94 hours, 30 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, followed some 10 minutes later with the commander going over; Tom Stafford.
Heart rates and respiration rates are up slightly above what they were at LOS on the - or Loss Of Signal - at the 8th revolution.
Still awaiting word from the surgeon as to whether the men are awake or not.

Surgeon reported to the Flight Director Pete Frank here, that it appears that the crew is still in light sleep; Flight Director said, "We'll leave them alone for a while." Next Loss Of Signal as the spacecraft goes around the back side of the Moon again on this revolution will be some 1 hours, 8 minutes from now.
Still no conversation with the spacecraft; we'll come back up when the communications do start for the day's activities, and at 92 hours, 35 minutes, Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 92 hours, 51 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We are anticipating a wake-up call to the crew of Apollo 10, which will be in the form of music again, apparently.
Standing by for music to go up to the crew; pleasant way to start the day.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: ("The Best Is Yet to Come" played here.)
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I didn't think that sounded like the Marine Corps Hymn there, the music that was coming up.
But it did sound pretty good.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go ahead and have your breakfast, and we're ready to go when you are.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're a bit ahead of schedule.
We've already had breakfast and gone through the redundant component check.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: ECS PERIODIC REDUNDANT COMPONENT CHECKLISTMain O2regulatorsMAIN REG B vlv on panel 351 - closeEMER CABIN PRESS vlv on panel 351 - 1PUSH TO TEST PB-PUSH (O2 FLOW INC) alongside the EMER CABIN PRESS valve on panel 351O2 press - 90-110 psig (from MSFN if avail)MAIN REG B vlv on panel 351- openMAIN REG A vlv on panel 351- closeEMER CABIN PRESS vlv on panel 351- 2PUSH TO TEST PB - PUSH (O2 FLOW INC) alongside the EMER CABIN PRESS valve on panel 351O2 press - 90-110 psig (from MSFN if avail)MAIN REG A vlv on panel 351- open.EMER CABIN PRESS vlv on panel 351- BOTH (OFF if suited)Secondary Glycol LoopOpen cool atten panel (If req'd)EVAP H2O CONT SEC vlv - AUTO on panel 382ECS IND sw - SEC on panel 2SEC COOL LOOP - AC 1 (AC 2) on panel 2GLY DISCH SEC PRESS - 39-51 psig meter on panel 2ACCUM SEC QTY IND - 30-55% meter on panel 2SEC COOL LOOP EVAP - EVAP on panel 2SEC EVAP STEAM PRESS .1-.15 boiling >.16 not boiling meter on panel 2After 5 minSEC EVAP TEMP OUT - 40-50.5 deg FSEC COOL LOOP EVAP - RESET for 58 sec minimum then off (ctr) on panel 2ECS IND sw - PRIM on panel 2SEC COOL LOOP PUMP- OFF (ctr) on panel 2EVAP H2O CONT SEC vlv - OFF on panel 382
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We have a consumables update and Flight Plan update when you are ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Your consumables update valid at 93 hours; your RCS total was 77 per cent: quad A, 75 per cent; quad B, 81 per cent; quad C, 77 per cent; D, 81 per cent...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The geometrically opposite SM RCS quadrants are displaying a good propellant balance, indicating good performance and propellant management.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Go ahead, Houston, with the consumable and Flight Plan update, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We read you loud - We read you loud and clear, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We read you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Valid at 93 hours: RCS total, 77 per cent; quad A, 70 - 75 per cent; quad B, 81 per cent; quad Charlie, 77 per cent; quad Delta, 81 per cent.
That's 8 per cent ahead of your Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I have a Flight Plan update when you are ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: At the end of your postsleep checklist, add these items.
At 93 plus 45, terminate battery B charge.
At 93:50, dump waste water to 36 per cent.
All lunar orbit activities are about 12 minutes later than the Flight Plan.
I have your nominal burn times, if you want them.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: They are 12 minutes behind the Flight Plan timeline due to the slightly extended Trans Lunar Coast (TLC) duration.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll get that later, Jack, thank you.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That was terminate battery B charge on waking up and dump the waste water to 36 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: That's the end of the update.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You boys have been up a while, I see.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We tried to sneak up on it just by - just about a half hour or so.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you have some time to listen sometime when you are interested, I have the local newspaper, again.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We would like to listen to it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: One kind of interesting thing was John's horoscope this morning; says, "Everybody you know has something helpful to offer.
Listen carefully while you make the rounds quickly.
Put in a busy day and assemble your results in the evening." Now here goes the news.
Springfield, Massachusetts: students at Springfield Technical College told President Edmund T.
Garvey they were taking over the Administration Building.
The students, about 40 in number, marched into the building Wednesday night armed with mops, brooms, scrub brushes, and staged a "clean-in." They said they would clean all night.
A student spokesman said the clean-in at the 1,200-member campus was to support the administrative policies of the 2-year-old school.
Safi, Morocco: on Friday, Thor Heyerdahl will set out from here to cross the Atlantic in a papyrus boat.
The man who must keep his papyrus boat together with rope and string bought his third wife this year, and is now complaining about the price.
She cost about 60 dollars in Egypt, much more than the going rate in Chad, where Abu Debrine learned how to make papyrus boats and hitched onto Heyerdahl's expedition.

If he succeeds in reaching Mexico in his boat, modeled after a 4,700-year-old Egyptian craft, Heyerdahl will consider he has strengthened the argument that the great early civilization of the Americas learned from the Pharaohs.
Debrine is packing pictures of wives A and B, smiling side by side, into his kit for the reed boat trip.
A photo of wife C, who has less seniority but is more expensive, gets a less prominent place.

Paris: Allied negotiators headed into the Vietnam talks today with what sources close to the meeting said were optimistic that discussions of proposals by President Nixon and the Viet Cong could bring progress.
delegation sources said Henry Cabot Lodge, Chief U.S.
negotiator, would comment, on the Viet Cong's 10-point peace plan Washington said included some points meriting further study.
The chief North Vietnamese negotiator indicated he and his Viet Cong counterpart were still studying the Nixon eight-point proposal.
Burger, an Appeals Court judge with a reputation for being strong on law and order, was picked Wednesday by President Nixon to be Chief Justice of the United States.
Burger, 61, a member of the U.S.
Circuit Court of Appeals in Washington since he was appointed by President Dwight D.
Eisenhower in 1956, is known as a strict constructionist, the type of judge Nixon promised to elevate to the court during his campaign, last fall.
Plymouth, England: solo around the world sailor, Nigel Tetley, was pulled from the Atlantic by a tanker crew today when his boat sank 14 days from home.
Tetley's wife, on hearing the news, said, "It is our home that is gone.
All my pots and pans have gone to the bottom of the sea." Tetley was competing in a global race sponsored by a London newspaper.
Moscow: Moscow TV showed the Apollo 10 astronauts in a 1-minute broadcast.
It said it was live from the American space capsule.
And do you remember that unemployed local philosopher?
He now says that while he believes in the future of color television, he thinks that because of your flight, it will go round and round in people's minds for a while yet.
Here is a sports story: Houston 3, Montreal 2, and Houston has just climbed out of the cellar.
New York 5, Atlanta 0; Chicago at Los Angeles, a night game; just heard from the back room that Los Angeles beat Chicago.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We've got a couple of items here we'd like your help on.

We have a temperature rise in the helium tank in quad Alpha, so what we'd like you to do after LOS, we'd like you to roll 180 degrees and then come back to the normal attitude at AOS for S-band acquisition.

In addition, we'd like your crew status report, and we'd like you to include some information on the cabin environment during the night to see if there is any change in it since the evaporators were not on the line.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We didn't need to turn off all that stuff.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Could we have an update on when you expect LOS?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: LOS is going to be at 93:42, and AOS will be at 94:29.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: On that roll, do you want a roll to 180 degrees, or roll a Delta angle of 180 degrees?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: 10, we want you to roll a Delta angle of 180 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Young's question is whether MCC-H want a change (Delta) of 180 degrees in roll or a manoeuvre to the 180 degrees position in roll.

This attitude change is to put SM RCS quadrant Alpha into the shade to reduce it's temperature whilst the spacecraft is out of contact behind the Moon, returning to the nominal attitude just prior to AOS so that they are in the right attitude for immediate reacquisition via the S-band antenna.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's what I figured; just trying to clarify it, though.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Jack, the ECS redundant component check is complete, and it looks good from here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, crew status report: We all had about six pretty good hours of sleep.
We've eaten breakfast this morning, and the readings on the dosimeters are, in order, 26037, 05307, 15039.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The cryo fans have been cycled, redundant component check is complete.
I'm about ready to purge the H2here in about 5 minutes, and then we'll get the battery and the water dump here before too long.
I might take the batteries off the line, that charge off the line, maybe 10 - 15 minutes early, if it's compatible with our suiting up and everything.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And the battery action is all right by us.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'd like you to cycle the heaters on the hydrogen cryo tanks; number 1, Off; number 2, Auto.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And I'm ready to purge the fuel cells.
I'll start with fuel cell 3, oxygen then hydrogen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apparently the crew is busy at the present time doing some housekeeping chores, washing the breakfast dishes, and so on.

The wakeup call which turned out not to be a wakeup at all because the crew had already been awake and had had breakfast, was playing up to the crew Tony Bennett's record of "The Best Is Yet To Come." The crew reported they all had 6 hours of good solid sleep.

They gave their radiation dosimeter readings on all three crewmen, and from the ground they were given Flight Plan updates of a few changes in the Flight Plan, moving a few operations around slightly.
We're still 23 minutes away from Loss Of Signal on this the 9th revolution around the Moon.
We'll continue to monitor the air-to-ground and leave the circuit live until we do have Loss Of Signal.
Ground Elapsed Time is now 93 hours, 19 minutes.
Acquisition Of Signal, the next revolution, 10th revolution, will be at 94 hours, 28 minutes, 50 seconds.

The crew has not as yet been passed up the new times for the maneuver sequence, all the maneuvers that have to go into the rendezvous such as the Descent Orbit Insertion, the phasing, coelliptic sequence, constant delta height, etc.
These likely will be read up to them during the next revolution.
Apollo Control standing by on air-ground for resumption of communications with Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: I have a map update for rev 10 if you want it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hold it just a minute, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, the fuel cell purge is complete, the H2purge line heater is Off, and I'd like to go ahead and terminate battery B charging at the time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The purge line which carries H2to the fuel to purge them has an in-line heater.
Just prior to a fuel cell purge the heater is switched.
The cryogenic temperature of the H2could cause the water by-product to freeze in this line if it was not heated.
The fuel cell itself creates sufficient heat from the power generation reaction to avoid the cells themselves freezing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stand by one on the batt.
You are clear to terminate battery charge.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Termination of the routine charging of the CSM entry battery B.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'd like you to verify that all the fans are off in the cryo tanks.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll turn them off at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I got too many meters running, I guess, right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, give me another hack on when LOS is, would you, and AOS?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I have your map updates for 10 and 11 when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The map update times are given for each revolution and are hand written on the Flight Plan.
The times given for Rev 10 are LOS, 93:42:40; sunrise, 93:52:22; crossing 150°W, 93:54:35; AOS, 94:28:50; sunset, 95:04:06.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, Jack I got all those and good, I don't - I don't need to read them back.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think we're in pretty good shape except for the waste-water dump which we've got yet to go right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And you're dumping to 36 per cent.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Houston, we're starting the waste-water dump now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We wanted to remind you to actuate your GDC, optics power and potable water.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Optics power coming on right now and potable water, GDC On.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The G&N Optics Power switch on panel 100 has two positions.
In the On position supplies DC operating power to the optics subsystem via the Power and Servo Assembly (PSA) on both main buses.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Att Set switch on panel 1 is set to GDC (Gyro Display Coupler) prior to GDC alignment.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Pot H2O Htr on panel 2, is switched off as part of the pre-sleep procedure.
No as part of the post sleep procedures the heater is turned back on again.
It provides 28 VDC to the potable water tank heater via the selected main bus.
The heater is required to warm the water being provided as a bi-product of the reaction in the fuel cells.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're going to lose you around the corner.
We'll pick you up in about 46 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control, 1 minute to Loss Of Signal as Apollo 10 goes around behind the Moon in the 9th revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: To give you a status, we've got two guys - one suited - John's getting suited.
I'm on my way to the LM here as soon as we complete the waste-water dump.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We have had Loss Of Signal with Apollo 10.

The crewmen who will go aboard the LM, the Commander and the Lunar Module Pilot, are now preparing to repressurize the LM, go in through the tunnel, power up all the LM systems, complete the checkout, and prepare for the day's activities which will be about an 8-hour sequence of several maneuvers in lunar orbit to simulate everything but an actual landing, the lunar landing mission that will be carried out on Apollo 11.
Some 44 minutes to acquisition of Apollo 10 on the next revolution.
That will be at 94 hours, 28 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
And at 93 hours, 44 minutes Ground Elapsed Time; this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Apollo 10 begins its tenth lunar orbit at about 093:50:00
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The source of electrical power is now transferred entirely to the LM batteries.
Up to this point, the CSM has provided an alternative source for the LM systems.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is commenting on the current roll attitude that Young has stopped at.
It is at 330 degrees instead of the prescribed 315 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is commenting on the SM RCS Helium tank supply pressure and temperature.
It is displayed on a meter on panel 2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is again commenting on the SM RCS Helium tank supply pressure and temperature.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford transfers into the LM Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control; 94 hours, 29 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
We should have Acquisition Of Signal now in the 10th revolution as Apollo 10 comes around from behind the Moon.
We should, for the first time, begin hearing the call signs,Snoopyfor the Lunar Module and here...
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Tom and Gene are in the LM, checking things out.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've got the LM power on.
LM cables are disconnected and stowed.
I and Tom are in our PGAs, and you got the roll CAL from last night.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We'd like to have the CMC for an uplink.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I read you guys loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: You have P00 and Accept, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We just got a VHF B check and it's so much better than the simulator, Tom says you can't believe it, and I agree.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, Snoopy is giving you a call on S-band.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: They are reading you, there, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Houston, Snoopy is calling you and he reads you apparently.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We are not reading you yet.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The LM backup voice transmission is accommodated via S-band by routing the low pass filter output directly to a speech processing network.
This network provides 24db of speech clipping.
This mode is set by setting the Biomed Telemetry switch on panel 12 to Off and the Voice Functions switch to Dn Voice BU.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: He wants to know how you read, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Hello, Houston, Houston, this is Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hello there Snoopy this is Houston.
Reading you now, but quite a bit of noise in the background.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: How do you read me now, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't hear the noise now, but you're way down in the mud.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The noise was because I was on ICS (intercommunication system) PTT (push to talk) Downvoice Backup and you had a high Freq in here.
I'll bring you up to date.
You might get your activation and checkout list out and wait a second - I'll Tell you where we are.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: are you done - with ECS second?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, be advised you are still down in the mud.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm yelling as loud as I can.
I just finished the LGC self-test.
The spacecraft is all Go; I found nothing wrong with it.
I'm ready to do the S-band TR power amplifier check, and then I will do the steerable check, and I will go out and get suited while Tom continues.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm presently on high voltage taps, and I'm ready to go S-band transmitter/receiver secondary and power amplifier secondary.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think I've got your helmet here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Go ahead and I'll wait 60 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: John, do I have your helmet?
I don't want the cover on it.
How about giving me one of those other helmets.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: How do you read on Secondary?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We have a lot of noise in the background; hearing you very weakly.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're loud and clear to us.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I read you loud and clear, I'll go back to Primary at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger; Hear you're going back to Primary.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: As part of the S-band transmitter/receiver power amplifier checks, Cernan is selecting the Secondary power amplifier using the switch on panel 12.
The quality of the comm via the Secondary power amplifier was not acceptable so he is now switching to the Primary power amplifier in an attempt to improve the quality.
The power amplifier circuit breaker on panel 16 would already have been closed.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom, are you checking these things off?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm squared - I'm waiting for - to get John on VHF A Simplex down there on the list.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Are you done with the suit fan check?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm ready to do the S-band steerable antenna check.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hello, Houston, this is Snoopy, I'm ready to do the S-band steerable check.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Snoopy is ready to do the steerable S-band antenna check.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Request you try turning your ranging switch Off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: They want you to try turning your ranging switch Off, Gene-o.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: This is Snoopy with the ranging switch Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The S-Band Function-Range switch on panel 12, in the Off/Reset position inhibits range data transmission to MSFN.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Gene, We're still hearing you very weakly, and a lot of background noise, over.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I don't doubt about the background noise.
We got it in here, but I can't do anything except eat these mikes from here on out
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm ready to go with the steerable test, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Go ahead with the steerable check.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Hey, John, are you in the attitude as prescribed?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're still reading Snoopy very weakly with a lot of background noise.
You may have to relay some.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And I should have good high gain lock.
How do you read on high gain?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, that's much better now, Snoop.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I don't know if that's any sign.
That was a piece of cake.
I hope it works that way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're coming in loud and clear now, Snoopy.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: things are going, so far, real well up in here.
I'm about ready to go off the loop here, go back and get suited up, and we'd like to stay ahead of the game if we can.
All my voltages look real good.
Just to bring you up to date: I'm, reading 30.2 on the commander's bus and 30.2 on the systems engineer's bus.
Inverter number 2 is in the high side of the Green.
Battery number 6 is reading 37 and battery number 5 is reading 37.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The LMP, Eugene Cernan has entered the LMSnoopyfirst to commence the systems activation, commencing with the Electrical Power Subsystem (EPS).
Meanwhile, CDR, Thomas Stafford has remained in the more spacious CM where he has donned his pressure garment with the assistance of CMP, John Young.
The LMP and CDR are about to swap roles, temporarily.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is commenting on the voltage of the two ascent stage batteries, 5 and 6.
They are both currently displaying 37 volts DC.
The inverter switch on panel 14 has been set to inverter 2 and is displaying the AC bus voltage on the Volts indicator also on panel 14.
It is currently indicating at the top of the scale.
The inverter output to the AC bus is regulated at 115 volts AC.
Should the AC voltage fall below 112 volts DC, the Inverter caution light is illuminated on panel 2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And our glycol temperature started out at about 70 or 75 and it's come down very slowly, and is now within the green band.
I guess it's about 50 - 48 degrees right now.
Our SHe pressure looks good, within the nominal limits; and our ambient pressure looks good; and our ascent helium pressures look good, also.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The primary glycol coolant loop temperature is being read from the indicator on panel 2.
The temperature will normally settle out at 40 degrees F, it is cooling down from the initial high temperature as the pumps circulate the coolant to the cold plates and rails.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The descent propellant pressurisation is provided by supercritical helium (SHe) and ambient helium.
The ambient helium provides prepessurisation of the descent propellants.
The supercritical helium cannot be used for the prepessurisation because the helium circulating through the fuel/helium heat exchanger may freeze the fuel before the fuel flow is established.
The supercritical helium is introduced after DPS ignition.

Both the SHe and ambient helium tank pressures are monitored by selecting the required output on the Helium Mon(itor) rotary selector switch on panel 1 which will then display the reading on the Helium indicator also on panel 1.
The initial ambient pressure should be on the order of 1,600 psia at 70 deg F (21 deg C), and the SHe pressure is nominally 1,555 psia at -400°F (-240°C).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The ascent engine propellants are pressurised by gaseous helium supplied from two tanks.
The nominal pressure is 3,050 psia at 70°F (21°C).
The ascent helium tank pressures can be monitor again by using the Helium Mon rotary selector switch on panel 1, selecting the appropriate tank and displaying the output on the Helium indicator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Jack, the next time I see you, I'll be dressed for the occasion.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll be looking forward to it, and we'd also like to keep ahead a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Lousma is encouraging the crew to try and stay ahead of the Flight Plan time line, so as to have some time available to deal with any issues that may occur, especially considering this is only the second test flight of the LM and the first in lunar orbit.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm going back to suit up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Charlie Brown, we have a Landing Site 2 track pad when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And we've got a 1921 and a 130.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The Landing Site 2 landmark tracking update is recorded on the Flight Plan as shown below.130(in Mare Tranquillitatis)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: In two months' time, Mike Collins, CMP for Apollo 11, will use crater 130 as part of the navigation that will aid Lunar Module Eagle to the surface.
The larger (2.8 km) crater adjacent to 130 was formerly called Sabine D but is now called Collins.
It is shown in this image from the Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter spacecraft.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Snoop would like to do a VHF A Simplex check now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The VHF system is only used for voice communications between the two spacecraft and for ranging by the CSM.
The VHF checks are carried out to confirm the correct system configuration in both spacecraft.
The VHF B system has already been checked out satisfactorily.
The following sequence is followed to configure the LM and test the VHF system:
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: On panel 12:VHF A XTMR switch Voice, VHF A RCVR switch On, VHF B XTMR switch Data, TLM PCM switch Lo, Comm Ant: VHF select FWD, at the same time the CSM selects VHF Ant-SM Left.Unstow the LMP outboard armrest to provide access to the LMP-ACA (Attitude Controller Assembly).Audio: VHF A switch T/R (transmit / receive), Comm: TLM Biomed switch Off (the crewman should ensure the comm cable is not powered)Request CSM remove transfer umbilical suit (used during IVT (Intravehicular transfer) between CM and LM by CDR and LMP) power.When headset sound has ceased, disconnect the transfer umbilical comm cable from the PGA (Pressure Garment Assembly) and connect the LM comm cable to the PGA (transferring voice comm source from CM to LM).On panel 16:CB (circuit breaker) Comm: SE Audio close (push in), this supplies power to the crewman's comm carrier.Perform VHF comm check with CMP in CSM, using the PTT on the ACA.LM Attitude Controller Assembly.Perform comm check with CMP in CSM, using the PTT/keyer on the umbilical.PTT/keyer on the PGA electrical umbilical assembly.Request verification of CSM reception of LM LBR (low bit rate) data, if desired.

(The CSM cannot verify this LBR data onboard so it must be recorded on the CM DSE, the recorded data dumped to MSFN (Manned Spaceflight Network) where it is again recorded on the ground and the data verified.).On panel 12:Audio: Mode switch VOX (voice operated transmission)Perform comm check with CSM using VOX, the audio volume, VOX sensitivity and the squelch settings should be adjusted at this time to suit the mission conditions.Audio: Mode switch ICS/PTTLM communications controls - Panel 12.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm reading you loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

I'm reading you loud and clear.

transcript.speaker: Stafford

transcript.speaker: Young

transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Verb 16 displays the time on registers R1 in hrs, R2 in mins & R3 in secs to two decimal points, Noun 65 samples the AGC time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're in good shape, here, until we get an update.
Let's go, and give me Verb 05 Noun 01 1706, Enter.
See if that's changed and give me the numbers, babe.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Verb 05 Noun 01 1706 Enter displays TEphem(time of ephemeris update) on R1, R2 and R3.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: TEphemis a time value (24 bit time, expressed in centiseconds) which is the difference between the UTC time used for reference and the UTC lift-off time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If you have high gain lock, I'm ready for my E-memory dump on page 33.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Put your update link to Data, and we are ready for the E-memory dump.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Updata link to Data and E-memory dump coming at you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: To initialise the E-memory dump the crew type Verb 74 Enter on the LM DSKY.
This dumps the contents of all eight banks of the LGC memory for downlinking to MCC-H.
The dump is automatically repeated to ensure the data is received correctly by MSFN.
MCC-H can check the entire contents of the E-memory to establish if the LGC is operating correctly.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm ready to do that docked alignment - the docked alignment if you are.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: When you get all squared away, we go in the minimum deadband attitude hold, hold the rascal tight; and then on your mark if you can give me Verb 06 Noun 20, and then just hold it until I get my alignment squared away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Verb 06 Noun 20 in the CMC displays the present ICDU (Inertial Coupling Data Unit) angles, in roll, pitch and yaw to two decimal points on registers 1, 2 and 3.
The 3 ICDU's provide an interface between the inertial subsystem and the computer.
It functions primarily as an analog-to-digital or digital-to-analog converter.
It converts IMU gimbal angles from analog-to-digital form, and supplies the CMC with this information.
Stafford will be using these angles as part of the LM IMU coarse align.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I'm going to SCS and then deadband.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: If you're in Min deadband attitude hold and when it all settles down there, give me Verb 06 Noun 20, and we'll have at it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Reading them back: number 1, 13807; number 2, 20094; number 3, 00055.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The ICDU angles Young has passed to Stafford are: roll, 138.07°; Pitch, 200.94°; Yaw, 000.55°.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Hold it there and we will be at it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We have your DAP update PAD when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: John - John, you can go ahead and release it, the minimum deadband.
We're all out on the course align and we're squared away.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The ICDU angles passed from the CSM 'Charlie Brown' need to be amended by manual calculations to take into account the difference in the orientation of the IMU's in the two spacecraft.
Additionally the docking roll calibration angle must be read off the docking tunnel scribed scale and factored in to these calculations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The calculation made is:OGA LM = 300° + Rc - OGA CMIGA LM = 180° + IGA CMMGA LM = 360° - MGA CM(OGA = Outer gimbal angle.
MGA = Middle gimbal angle for the appropriate spacecraft.)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew in the LMSnoopyenters the resultant angles into the LGC by keying Verb 41 Noun 20 Enter.
The DSKY will flash Verb 21 Noun 22 requesting the crew to key in the angles in each axes to drive the LM IMU gimbals to this orientation.
Whilst the CDR is performing this procedure in the LM, CMP John Young has set the DAP to deadband to narrow band ±0.5° so that the combined spacecraft orientation is kept within tight limits.
Now that the coarse alignment is complete the minimum deadband can be opened to wideband ±5.0° to reduce the amount of CSM RCS activity.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Go back to CMC in Auto.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And real fast here: if you can give me a Verb 06 Noun 20, we'll mark it forward and call the ground on a fine align.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let me know and I'll give the Enter on Verb 06 Noun 20.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I was a little late on that one.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: CDR Stafford has request a second readout of the ICDU angles so he can confirm the gimbal angles he has entered.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Did you read out that John's Verb 06 Noun 20?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We got you: plus 16070, plus 02023,plus 30 - correction - plus 35973.

tokens: CDR Stafford has now read up to MCC-H the IMU gimbal angles for them to be checked on the ground.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

transcript.speaker: Stafford

transcript.speaker: Young

If you read my DSKY, I've got it three balls 12, 13256, 33266.

transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: For Charlie Brown: this is VHF/AM A Simplex basic check.
Your VHF/AM A switch in Simplex, VHF/AM B in Off.
VHF/AM receive only in Off, VHF ranging Off.
And for Snoopy: VHF transmitter A to Voice, VHF receiver A to On, and on the commander's audio center your VHF A transmit/receive to TR.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: For Snoopy, that was VHF A transmit voice receiver On, and what was the last one?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: VHF receiver A to On, and on the commander's audio side, the VHF A TR to TR, and check all circuit breakers In.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: From theApollo 10 Final Flight Evaluation Report:"At 94 hours, 46 minutes, the commander attempted a transmission on Simplex-A; however, the circuit breaker supplying power for the keying relay was open, rendering VHF Simplex-A inoperative.
Transmission on Simplex-A was attempted again at 95 hours 16 minutes.
A check of switch positions for both spacecraft was performed.
Both LM crewmen attempted unsuccessfully to transmit on Simplex-A.
The commander then tried Simplex-B with no success; however, his Simplex-B switch had been left in the 'Receive' position from the previous check, and he could not transmit at the time.
The commander then switched to 'Transmit/Receive' and Simplex-B performed satisfactorily.
With the pressure of time, the crew decided to use Simplex-B.

However, during the backside pass of revolution 11, VHF Simplex-A was tried again, and it performed satisfactorily The 'A' transmitter was used for both voice and ranging for the remainder of the flight.
The most probable cause for the apparent failures of VHF Simplex-A was the numerous switch configuration changes in both vehicles.
The two vehicles were not configured simultaneously for communications on Simplex-A."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom, you got - commander, are you on T/R?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stafford is confirming the VHF system A and B settings on his audio panel 8.

transcript.speaker: Stafford

Stafford now confirms that the probe capture latches are in place an engaged then he closes the LM upper hatch.

They keep - they keep coming out.
Now, it looks like you got them.

When we just tried it again, it had been in the Off position.

The backup procedure is to use the LM rendezvous radar to range the CSM.

tokens: That hatch good and clean, babe?

He would rather make this manoeuvre whilst the docking latches are still in place.
The Flight Plan indicates that this manoeuvre should take place prior to disengaging the docking latches.

tokens: This freaking Velcro in this - both of these machines is horrible!
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew repeatedly bemoaned the functioning of the Velcro patches in both spacecraft and on their overalls and pressure suits.
Pockets would open and tools float out, restraints to keep the crews feet on the LM floor would fail.
They commented that the Velcro needed to have a higher shear strength to be effective.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And this ECS system isn't much better than what we squawked at in the altitude chamber.
Soon as I start puffing I start breathing those - Gene-o, you want to give me one quick - push here?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: You're not in a - in that Receive position...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Ah, shit I always try to leave it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We've got helmets and gloves on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: CMP John Young has reinstalled the docking probe.

He has preloaded the probe using the extend latch assembly to assert a load through the probe to drogue to ensure the load on the pressure seals between the two spacecraft is maintained once the docking latches are disengaged.
LMP Eugene Cernan is giving CMP John Young the go ahead to retract the 12 docking latches.
He releases them one at a time and cocks them in readiness for the next docking operation.
He now reinstalls the CM forward hatch and performs a hatch integrity check to ensure a proper pressure seal.
The two spacecraft are now being held together only by the docking probe capture latches engaged in the drogue.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I've got the Cabin Gas Return to Egress...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The Cabin Gas Return valve is situated on the Suit Circuit And Canister Controls panel.
It provides for return of cabin gas to the suit circuit under normal operations or provides suit integrity under rapid cabin decompression.
In the Egress position, the valve is closed, ensuring the integrity of the suit circuit during unpressurised LM cabin operations.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Don't we want to close that thing, Tom?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Tell him I'm going to close the hatch now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we're closing our hatch at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: LMP is now closing and securing the overhead LM tunnel hatch.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's about as clean as you can get it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: When this goes to Auto, let's make sure that we pull that thing down, after our check.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When we go to Auto on that...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I'll disable the roll jets here.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Tom, put these up there where they belong now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll put my bag over on the - You want to take your bag out of the way?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I guess we should really go to Auto here.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Our uplink is complete, and you have a Go for a maneuver to the landmark tracking attitude, and we have to have the OPS source pressure.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We've got to go to Auto.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let's see what it says here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: When he vents the tunnel, he's going to suck us down.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Overhead Dump valve to Auto, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Overhead Dump valve is situated on the LM overhead docking hatch.
It provides for automatic LM cabin depressurisation and cabin pressure relief.
In the Auto position, the valve can operate normally as an automatic pressure relief device.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Got to put that latch in there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Pressure Regulator A and B, Cabin.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Pressure Regulator A and B allows the Suit Press indicator to operate.
Both valves must be set simultaneously to accomplish the desired pressure regulation.
In the Cabin position, the regulated oxygen flow is permitted into the suit circuit at a maintained pressure of 4.8plus or minus 0.2 psia.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Suit Gas Diverter valve, Push, Cabin.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The Suit Gas Diverter valve can be closed automatically by a signal from the cabin pressure sensor with the cabin pressure drops below 4.0 psia.
The valve will also close automatically when the Press Reg A & B valves are set to Egress.
In the Push-Cabin position, permits gas in the suit circuit downstream of the flow control valves to flow into the cabin.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Okay, there's Cabin, Cabin - got an eye on Cabin...
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I've got the Cabin Gas Return, see, in case that thing blew out - that was open into our suit loop - the Cabin Gas Return.
Cabin, Cabin, everything is sealed up.
Cabin Gas Return is Auto and Suit Circuit Relief is Auto.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: With the Cabin Gas Return in the Auto position it permits gas to flow into, but not out of, the suit circuit.
The Suit Circuit Relief valve prevents overpressurisation of the suit circuit.
In Auto it permits the valve to respond to aneroid device measuring suit circuit pressure.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I read 5800 on both OPS's this morning when I came in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The OPS (Oxygen Purge System) is part of the Apollo EMU (Extravehicular Mobility Unit).
It provides a backup source of oxygen should the primary supply from the PLSS (Portable Life Support System) fail or be expended.
The OPS would also be used to supply oxygen to the LM crew should they need to make a contingency transfer between the LM and the CSM, going EVA and entering the CM via the main hatch.
The pressure of the oxygen stored in the OPS should be at 5880 psia ±500psia.
The readout Cernan has given indicates the OPS are in good health and ready for use if required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Tom, will these things snap back there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do we have those gyro torquing angles?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Let me snap these things down out of the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I recommend you lock your high gain antenna.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: The LM S-band steerable antenna has to be turned off when not in use.
This prevents equipment damage due to possible overheating if the antenna drives to the stop after loss of phase lock due to Loss Of Signal with MSFN as the combined spacecraft go behind the Moon.
This is done by placing the Track Mode switch on panel 12 to the Off position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Did you get those numbers, Tom?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They can take the Velcro that's on these spacecraft and - I don't know what the hell they can do with them, but they can give it back.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We notice an increase in your O2flow.
Recommend two return valves Closed with your helmets and gloves off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Young, from the1969 Technical debrief:"When I removed my helmet and gloves, I got an O2Flow High warning light; the reason for that was that the inflow valve had been closed as per checklist.
I opened the inflow valve, but you know I was kind of nervous, because it indicated that we had a pressure leak, and when I vented the tunnel, the tunnel would not vent."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young (continued):"...it was partially caused by the fact that it (inflow valve) was clogged by Mylar insulation that had been sucked in the second time and the other thing that caused it was that, when you have your helmet and gloves off with the inflow valve closed, you get a O2Flow High (warning light).
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've had Loss Of Signal as the Apollo 10 spacecraft went behind the Moon in this 10th revolution.
We've got about 45 minutes, 15 seconds until Acquisition Of Signal again.

We've got about 14 minutes of colour video tape to play back from the May 21st TV feed from the spacecraft to the Madrid tracking station, which was videotaped there and the tape flown to this country for a play back, and it was not relayed in real time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Young is checking the state of the docking latches.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is confirming that the LM overhead Cabin Relief and Dump Valve is in the Auto position.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is referring to the LM Tunnel Vent valve, the controls for which are on CM panel 12.
With the valve in the LM Tunnel Vent position, the LM tunnel will be depressurized, venting the tunnels atmosphere out into space.
This allows the integrity on the CM forward hatch pressure seals to be checked.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With the LM tunnel vent valve control in the LM/CM Delta-P position, the atmosphere in LM tunnel is connected to the Delta-P gauge, allowing the pressure differential betwen the LM tunnel and the CM cabin to be measured.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is getting exasperated by the rate the tunnel pressure is dropping.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Apollo 10 begins its eleventh lunar orbit at about 095:55:00
transcript.speaker: Commentary
